title: GA4 Analytics Engineering with R, Arrow & DuckDB & Parquet
text:
Google Analytics 4 (GA4) stores event data in deeply nested, hierarchical structures in BigQuery — a format optimized for collection, but challenging for analysis. This series of notebooks walks through the full analytics engineering workflow: from understanding the raw nested structure, to downloading and storing data efficiently, to answering real marketing questions at scale.






CautionCaution for Coding Accuracy



You are advised to be aware of potential inaccuracy of the coding provided on this site. Depending on the computer setup, IDE you used, packages you installed, how you want to save you your GA4 data, some adjusmtents are expected.
Also, due to limitations of publicl GA4 data, some data is blank (e.g., user properties), leading to no insights for the variables in the demonstration.








Warning



Working with large data makes you slow. Saving data in parquet solve the problem. However, GA4’s deeply nested data present challenges in saving them in your local computer. All the demonstrations were made on a relatively small GA4 data (i.e., 7 days) with about 400,000 rows of events. You are expected to save the file in eihter RDS or qs files on your computer.
Even though they are not big, it took very long time to flatten event params data when RDS or qs data were read into RAM.
To solve this problem, it is worth looking into Data Acquisition Guide for GA4 Public Data

text:
What You Will Learn

Rectangling — how to flatten nested list-columns using tidyr verbs like unnest_longer(), unnest_wider(), and hoist()
GA4-specific challenges — handling union-type value structs, key-value event_params arrays, and BigQuery INT64 types
Efficient storage — comparing RDS, qs2, Parquet, Arrow, and DuckDB for local caching of GA4 data
Large-scale analytics — using Arrow and DuckDB to query GA4 data without loading everything into RAM
Marketing analytics — translating business questions into R code using GA4 event data

title: Rectangling GA4 Hierarchical Data: Part I - Understanding GA4 Data
text:
0.1 What You Will Learn
This document bridges R for Data Science Chapter 23 (Rectangling) and the real-world complexity of Google Analytics 4 (GA4) data stored in BigQuery. You will learn to transform deeply nested, hierarchical event data into flat, analysis-ready tables — and then use those tables to answer genuine marketing questions.






NoteConnection to R4DS Chapter 23



This document follows the structure of R4DS Chapter 23: Hierarchical Data. Every tool introduced there — unnest_wider(), unnest_longer(), and hoist() — appears here applied to real GA4 data. Where GA4 requires techniques beyond what Chapter 23 covers, those gaps are explicitly flagged and supplemented.

0.2 Learning Objectives
By the end of this workshop you will be able to:

Explain why GA4 stores data in a hierarchical (nested) format in BigQuery
Connect R to BigQuery using {bigrquery} and {DBI}
Apply unnest_wider() to flatten GA4 struct columns (device, geo, traffic_source)
Apply unnest_longer() to expand GA4 repeated record columns (items)

Execute that 3-stage pipeline: unnest_longer() → coalesce() → pivot_wider()
Produce marketing analyses for traffic sources, devices, content, and e-commerce using the Google Merchandise Store public dataset

0.3 The Dataset: Google Merchandise Store (Obfuscated)
Google provides a free public GA4 dataset in BigQuery derived from their own online store (merch.google.com). The data covers real shopping behaviour — page views, product interactions, and purchases — but user identifiers have been obfuscated for privacy.







Detail
Value




BigQuery path
bigquery-public-data.ga4_obfuscated_sample_ecommerce


Date range
November 2020 – January 2021


Table format
One table per day: events_YYYYMMDD


Access

title: Rectangling GA4 Hierarchical Data: Part I - Understanding GA4 Data
section: 1.1 From Flat Files to Event Streams
text:
1.1 From Flat Files to Event Streams
Traditional web analytics tools exported flat CSV files: one row per session, one column per metric. GA4 takes a fundamentally different approach. It records every user interaction as an event, and because different event types carry completely different attributes, a flat structure is impractical.
Consider the variety:

A page_view event carries: page URL, page title, referrer, scroll depth
A purchase event carries: transaction ID, revenue, coupon codes, items purchased
A click event carries: link URL, link text, element ID

If these lived in a flat table, you would need hundreds of nullable columns. BigQuery’s nested and repeated field types solve this elegantly — but require us to “rectangle” the data before we can analyse it.
crumbs: Rectangling GA4 Hierarchical Data: Part I - Understanding GA4 Data

title: Rectangling GA4 Hierarchical Data: Part I - Understanding GA4 Data
text:
1.2 GA4’s Three Nesting Patterns
GA4 data uses three distinct patterns of nesting. Understanding which pattern applies to each column is the key to choosing the right rectangling tool:








GA4 Nesting Patterns and Their Rectangling Tools


Column(s)
BigQuery Type
R List-Column Shape
Rectangling Tool




device, geo, traffic_source, app_info
RECORD (struct)
List of named lists (1 per row)
unnest_wider()


items
RECORD REPEATED (array of named structs)
List of tibbles with named columns
unnest_longer() + unnest_wider()


event_params, user_properties
RECORD REPEATED (array of key-value structs)
List of tibbles with key + typed value columns
unnest_longer() + coalesce() + pivot_wider()1



1 Pattern 3 is not covered in R4DS Ch. 23 and is the main supplementary topic of this document.














TipVisual Schema



Below is a simplified view of one row of GA4 data. Each indented block represents a nested structure:
── event_date:         "20210101"          ← flat string
── event_name:         "purchase"          ← flat string
── user_pseudo_id:     "abc123..."         ← flat string
──┬ device:                                ← STRUCT (Pattern 1)
  ├── category:        "mobile"
  └── browser:         "Chrome"
──┬ geo:                                   ← STRUCT (Pattern 1)
  ├── country:         "United States"
  └── city:            "New York"
──┬ items: [...]                           ← ARRAY of STRUCTS (Pattern 2)
  ├── [0] item_id: "GGOEGAAX0104"
  │       item_name: "Google Tee"
  │       price: 22.99
  └── [1] item_id: "GGOEAAAB081014"
          item_name: "Google Cap"
          price: 15.99
──┬ event_params: [...]                    ← ARRAY of KEY-VALUE STRUCTS (Pattern 3)
  ├── [0] key: "transaction_id"
  │       value.string_value: "T-54321"
  ├── [1] key: "value"
  │       value.float_value: 38.98
  └── [2] key: "session_id"
          value.int_value: 1234567890

title: Rectangling GA4 Hierarchical Data: Part I - Understanding GA4 Data
text:
2.1 Authentication and Connection






ImportantBefore You Begin



You need a Google Cloud Platform (GCP) account with a project that has billing enabled. Querying the public dataset is free in terms of data costs (you won’t be charged for the data itself), but GCP bills for query processing. Small queries on this dataset cost fractions of a cent.

Go to console.cloud.google.com
Create a project (or use an existing one)
Enable the BigQuery API for that project
Copy your Project ID — you’ll need it below





Code
# ── Replace with your GCP Project ID ─────────────────────────────────────────
# Either set the environment variable: usethis::edit_r_environ()
# and add: GCP_PROJECT_ID=your-actual-project-id
# Or assign directly (less portable):
billing_project <- "GCP_PROJECT_ID"

# ── Authenticate ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# First run: opens a browser for OAuth. Subsequent runs: uses cached token.
# In Positron IDE, if the browser redirect fails, add: use_oob = TRUE
bq_auth()
# bq_auth(use_oob = TRUE)  # Uncomment if you see an OAuth redirect error

# ── Connect to the public GA4 dataset ─────────────────────────────────────────
con <- dbConnect(
  bigrquery::bigquery(),
  project = "bigquery-public-data",
  dataset = "ga4_obfuscated_sample_ecommerce",
  billing = billing_project
)

# Verify connection: list available tables
dbListTables(con)



2.1.1 My Own BigQuery Account: Do not use this

title: Rectangling GA4 Hierarchical Data: Part I - Understanding GA4 Data
text:
2.2 Downloading and Caching the Data
GA4 tables are large. For this workshop we download one week of events (~150,000 rows) once, then save locally to avoid re-querying BigQuery on every render.


Code
# ── Run this chunk ONCE. It saves data locally. ───────────────────────────────
dir.create("data", showWarnings = FALSE)

query_result <- bq_project_query(
  billing_project,
  "SELECT
     event_date,
     event_timestamp,
     event_name,
     event_params,
     user_pseudo_id,
     device,
     geo,
     traffic_source,
     ecommerce,
     items,
     user_properties
   FROM `bigquery-public-data.ga4_obfuscated_sample_ecommerce.events_*`
   WHERE _TABLE_SUFFIX BETWEEN '20210101' AND '20210107'"
)

# A tibble data
ga4_raw <- query_result |>
  bq_table_download(
    bigint = "numeric", # Convert INT64 to R numeric (sufficient for most analyses)
    page_size = 1000 # Download in batches of 10k rows
  )

saveRDS(ga4_raw, "data/ga4_raw.rds")
message("Saved ", nrow(ga4_raw), " rows to data/ga4_raw.rds")



Note: RDS stands for R Data Serialization



Code
# ── Load from local cache (use this for all subsequent analyses) ──────────────
ga4 <- readRDS("data/ga4_raw.rds")

3.2 Inspecting Individual List-Columns
Let us look inside each type of nested column:


Inspect the device struct column
# device is a STRUCT — each cell is a named list
ga4$device[[1]] # Look at the first row's device record


$category
[1] "desktop"

$mobile_brand_name
[1] "Microsoft"

$mobile_model_name
[1] "<Other>"

$mobile_marketing_name
[1] "<Other>"

$mobile_os_hardware_model
[1] NA

$operating_system
[1] "Windows"

$operating_system_version
[1] "Windows 10"

$vendor_id
[1] NA

$advertising_id
[1] NA

$language
[1] "en-us"

$is_limited_ad_tracking
[1] "No"

$time_zone_offset_seconds
[1] NA

$web_info
$web_info[[1]]
$web_info[[1]]$browser
[1] "<Other>"

$web_info[[1]]$browser_version
[1] "<Other>"




Inspect the items array column
# items is an ARRAY of STRUCTs — each cell is a tibble (may be empty for non-purchase events)
# Find a row that has items (a purchase event)
purchase_rows <- which(ga4$event_name == "purchase")
ga4$items[[purchase_rows[1]]] # First purchase event's items



  





Inspect the event_params key-value column
# event_params is an ARRAY of KEY-VALUE STRUCTs
# Each cell is a tibble with columns: key, value (and value sub-columns)
page_view_rows <- which(ga4$event_name == "page_view")
ga4$event_params[[page_view_rows[1]]] # First page_view event's params



  









NoteWhat You’re Seeing




device: A single named list — one value per field. This is Pattern 1.
items: A tibble with one row per purchased item. Columns are the item’s attributes (item_name, price, etc.). This is Pattern 2.
event_params: A tibble with one row per parameter, but instead of named attribute columns, it has just key and value. The value is itself a mini-struct with four typed sub-fields. This is Pattern 3— the most complex case.

title: Rectangling GA4 Hierarchical Data: Part I - Understanding GA4 Data
text:
4.1 What Is Rectangling?
Rectangling is the process of transforming a deeply nested hierarchical data structure (like a list-column) into a flat, rectangular data frame where every column contains atomic values and every row represents one observation.
The core insight from R4DS is that there are really only two fundamental operations:








Operation
When to use
tidyr function




“Widen” a struct
Each list element is a named list (one record with named fields)
unnest_wider()


“Lengthen” an array
Each list element is a vector/list of multiple things (an array)
unnest_longer()



Most complex cases combine both operations in sequence.
crumbs: Rectangling GA4 Hierarchical Data: Part I - Understanding GA4 Data

title: Rectangling GA4 Hierarchical Data: Part I - Understanding GA4 Data
text:
4.2 Pattern 1: Simple Structs with unnest_wider()
GA4’s device, geo, and traffic_source columns are all non-repeated RECORD types — each row contains exactly one record with a fixed set of named fields. This is the simplest nesting pattern.

4.2.1 The device Column


Code
device_data <- ga4 |>
  select(user_pseudo_id, event_name, event_date, device) |>
  unnest_wider(device) # ← expands named list into columns

glimpse(device_data) # 13 columns unnested


Rows: 245,209
Columns: 16
$ user_pseudo_id           <chr> "1003046.9452926974", "1003046.9452926974", "…
$ event_name               <chr> "first_visit", "page_view", "session_start", …
$ event_date               <chr> "20210101", "20210101", "20210101", "20210101…
$ category                 <chr> "desktop", "desktop", "desktop", "desktop", "…
$ mobile_brand_name        <chr> "Microsoft", "Microsoft", "Microsoft", "Micro…
$ mobile_model_name        <chr> "<Other>", "<Other>", "<Other>", "<Other>", "…
$ mobile_marketing_name    <chr> "<Other>", "<Other>", "<Other>", "<Other>", "…
$ mobile_os_hardware_model <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, N…
$ operating_system         <chr> "Windows", "Windows", "Windows", "Windows", "…
$ operating_system_version <chr> "Windows 10", "Windows 10", "Windows 10", "Wi…
$ vendor_id                <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, N…
$ advertising_id           <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, N…
$ language                 <chr> "en-us", "en-us", "en-us", "en-us", "en-us", …
$ is_limited_ad_tracking   <chr> "No", "No", "No", "No", "No", "No", "No", "No…
$ time_zone_offset_seconds <dbl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, N…
$ web_info                 <list> [["<Other>", "<Other>"]], [["<Other>", "<Oth…








The device list-column is replaced by individual columns (13 of them): category, mobile_brand_name, browser, operating_system, etc. Each column name matches the original struct field name.









Tipnames_sep for Avoiding Name Collisions




When unnesting multiple struct columns that might share field names, use names_sep to prefix each new column:

```{r}
# Prevents collisions if two structs both have, e.g., a "version" field
ga4 |>
  unnest_wider(device, names_sep = "_") |>   # → device_category, device_browser
  unnest_wider(geo,    names_sep = "_")       # → geo_country, geo_city
```




4.2.2 The geo Column


Code
geo_data <- ga4 |>
  select(user_pseudo_id, event_date, geo) |>
  unnest_wider(geo, names_sep = "_") # → geo_country, geo_city, geo_region

glimpse(geo_data)


Rows: 245,209
Columns: 8
$ user_pseudo_id    <chr> "1003046.9452926974", "1003046.9452926974", "1003046…
$ event_date        <chr> "20210101", "20210101", "20210101", "20210101", "202…
$ geo_continent     <chr> "Europe", "Europe", "Europe", "Europe", "Europe", "A…
$ geo_sub_continent <chr> "Western Europe", "Western Europe", "Western Europe"…
$ geo_country       <chr> "France", "France", "France", "France", "France", "I…
$ geo_region        <chr> "Auvergne-Rhone-Alpes", "Auvergne-Rhone-Alpes", "Auv…
$ geo_city          <chr> "(not set)", "(not set)", "(not set)", "(not set)", …
$ geo_metro         <chr> "(not set)", "(not set)", "(not set)", "(not set)", …




4.2.3 Unnesting Multiple Structs in One Pipeline

In practice, you usually want to unnest several struct columns at once:



Code
ga4_flat_structs <- ga4 |>
  select(
    event_date,
    event_name,
    user_pseudo_id,
    event_timestamp,
    device,
    geo,
    traffic_source
  ) |>
  unnest_wider(device, names_sep = "_") |>
  unnest_wider(geo, names_sep = "_") |>
  unnest_wider(traffic_source, names_sep = "_")

# Now all struct columns are flat
names(ga4_flat_structs)


 [1] "event_date"                      "event_name"                     
 [3] "user_pseudo_id"                  "event_timestamp"                
 [5] "device_category"                 "device_mobile_brand_name"       
 [7] "device_mobile_model_name"        "device_mobile_marketing_name"   
 [9] "device_mobile_os_hardware_model" "device_operating_system"

section: 4.3 Pattern 2: Arrays of Named Structs with unnest_longer() + unnest_wider()
text:
4.3 Pattern 2: Arrays of Named Structs with unnest_longer() + unnest_wider()
The items column contains e-commerce line items. Unlike device, this is a repeated RECORD — a purchase can have multiple items. Each row’s items cell contains a tibble with one row per item.
This is the pattern covered in R4DS Chapter 23 using gh_repos as the example: an array where each element is a named struct.

4.3.1 Ch23

When the list is unnamed, use unnest_longer()
When the list is name, use unnest_wider()



4.3.2 Understanding the Two-Step Process


Code
# Step 0: examine the items column for a purchase event
purchase_rows <- which(ga4$event_name == "purchase")
ga4 |>
  filter(row_number() == purchase_rows[1]) |>
  select(event_name, items) |>
  unnest(items) # Quick look



  









unnest() unnest automatically by using a combination of both unnest_wider and unnest_longer. It is not good for learning.
Compare the code above with the one below.





Code
ga4 |>
  filter(row_number() == purchase_rows[1]) |>
  select(event_name, items) |> # unnamed list
  unnest_longer(items) |> # tibble: 1 × 2 named list
  unnest_wider(items) # tibble: 1 × 27



  




The pipeline for an array of named structs:

```{r}
items (list-column of tibbles)
  └─ unnest_longer(items)  → one row per item, items column now a single tibble-row
       └─ unnest_wider(items)  → each item field becomes its own column
```


Code
items_flat <- ga4 |>
  filter(event_name == "purchase") |>
  select(event_date, user_pseudo_id, event_timestamp, items) |>
  unnest_longer(items) |> # Step 1: one row per item
  unnest_wider(items) # Step 2: expand item struct fields into columns

text:
4.4 Pattern 1b: hoist() for Selective Extraction

When you only need a few fields from a large struct, hoist() is more efficient than a full unnest because it avoids creating columns you don’t need.







NoteR4DS Coverage




hoist() is covered in R4DS Section 23.4. The syntax differs slightly from unnest_wider()
You name the output columns and specify the path to each value.





Code
# Extract only country and device category without unnesting everything
ga4_targeted <- ga4 |>
  select(event_name, event_date, user_pseudo_id, device, geo) |>
  #unnest_wider(device)
  hoist(device, device_category = "category", browser = "browser") |>
  #unnest_wider(geo)
  hoist(geo, country = "country", city = "city")

glimpse(ga4_targeted)

$ device_category <chr> "desktop", "desktop", "desktop", "desktop", "desktop",…
$ browser         <lgl> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA…
$ device          <list> ["Microsoft", "<Other>", "<Other>", NA, "Windows", "W…

title: Rectangling GA4 Hierarchical Data: Part I - Understanding GA4 Data
text:
5.1 The Anatomy of event_params

In R4DS, the nested data examples (like got_chars or gh_repos) have named fields at each level. When you unnest a gh_repo, you get columns like id, name, and stargazers_count because those are the field names in the struct.
GA4’s event_params is fundamentally different. Instead of named fields, it stores parameters in a generic key-value format:

```{r}
# What we WISH event_params looked like (named struct — covered by R4DS):
event_params = { page_location = "...", session_id = 123, engagement_time = 456 }

# What event_params ACTUALLY looks like (key-value struct — NOT in R4DS):
event_params = [
  { key = "page_location",   value = { string_value = "https://...", int_value = NULL, ... } },
  { key = "ga_session_id",   value = { string_value = NULL, int_value = 123,            ... } },
  { key = "engagement_time", value = { string_value = NULL, int_value = 456,            ... } }
]
```
Why did Google design it this way? Because different event types send completely different parameters. Using a generic key-value store means the schema never changes — new parameters can be added to any event without altering the table structure.
The second complication: the value field is itself a struct with four sub-fields — one for each data type that a parameter value might be:
```{r}
value.string_value  ← populated if the value is text
value.int_value     ← populated if the value is an integer
value.float_value   ← populated if the value is a decimal
value.double_value  ← populated if the value is a high-precision decimal
```

For any given parameter, exactly one of these will be non-NULL. We must consolidate them into a single value column.
crumbs: Rectangling GA4 Hierarchical Data: Part I - Understanding GA4 Data

title: Rectangling GA4 Hierarchical Data: Part I - Understanding GA4 Data
text:
5.2 Step-by-Step: The Three-Stage Pipeline

5.2.1 Toy Example — Building Intuition
Before applying to real data, let us build the intuition with a small hand-crafted example:


Toy example illustrating the key-value problem
# Simulate what a single event's event_params looks like as a tibble
toy_params <- tibble(
  event_id = c(1L, 1L, 1L, 2L, 2L),
  event_name = c("page_view", "page_view", "page_view", "purchase", "purchase"),
  event_params = list(
    # event 1
    tibble(
      key = c("page_location", "ga_session_id", "engaged_session_event"),
      value = list(
        list(
          string_value = "https://shop.example.com/",
          int_value = NA,
          float_value = NA,
          double_value = NA
        ),
        list(
          string_value = NA,
          int_value = 7654321L,
          float_value = NA,
          double_value = NA
        ),
        list(
          string_value = NA,
          int_value = 1L,
          float_value = NA,
          double_value = NA
        )
      )
    ),
    # event 1 (second copy — same row)
    tibble(
      key = c("page_location", "ga_session_id", "engaged_session_event"),
      value = list(
        list(
          string_value = "https://shop.example.com/",
          int_value = NA,
          float_value = NA,
          double_value = NA
        ),
        list(
          string_value = NA,
          int_value = 7654321L,
          float_value = NA,
          double_value = NA
        ),
        list(
          string_value = NA,
          int_value = 1L,
          float_value = NA,
          double_value = NA
        )
      )
    ),
    # event 1 (third copy)
    tibble(
      key = c("page_location", "ga_session_id", "engaged_session_event"),
      value = list(
        list(
          string_value = "https://shop.example.com/",
          int_value = NA,
          float_value = NA,
          double_value = NA
        ),
        list(
          string_value = NA,
          int_value = 7654321L,
          float_value = NA,
          double_value = NA
        ),
        list(
          string_value = NA,
          int_value = 1L,
          float_value = NA,
          double_value = NA
        )
      )
    ),
    # event 2
    tibble(
      key = c("transaction_id", "value", "currency"),
      value = list(
        list(
          string_value = "T-00042",
          int_value = NA,
          float_value = NA,
          double_value = NA
        ),
        list(
          string_value = NA,
          int_value = NA,
          float_value = 49.98,
          double_value = NA
        ),
        list(
          string_value = "USD",
          int_value = NA,
          float_value = NA,
          double_value = NA
        )
      )
    ),
    # event 2 (second copy)
    tibble(
      key = c("transaction_id", "value", "currency"),
      value = list(
        list(
          string_value = "T-00042",
          int_value = NA,
          float_value = NA,
          double_value = NA
        ),
        list(
          string_value = NA,
          int_value = NA,
          float_value = 49.98,
          double_value = NA
        ),
        list(
          string_value = "USD",
          int_value = NA,
          float_value = NA,
          double_value = NA
        )
      )
    )
  )
)

cat("Starting shape:", nrow(toy_params), "rows x", ncol(toy_params), "cols\n")


Starting shape: 5 rows x 3 cols


Toy example illustrating the key-value problem
cat("event_params column type:", class(toy_params$event_params), "\n")


event_params column type: list 


Toy example illustrating the key-value problem
toy_params



  





5.2.2 Stage 1: unnest_longer() — Expand the Array

The first stage turns the array into rows. Each event’s parameter list becomes individual rows, one per parameter:



Code
stage1 <- toy_params |>
  unnest_longer(event_params)

cat("After Stage 1:", nrow(stage1), "rows x", ncol(stage1), "cols\n")


After Stage 1: 15 rows x 3 cols


Code
stage1 |> select(event_id, event_name, event_params) #  15 rows x 3 cols



  




After this step, event_params is no longer a list of tibbles — it is now a single tibble-row per event parameter. But the value inside each row is still a nested named list.



5.2.3 Stage 1b: unnest_wider() — Expand the Inner Struct

Now flatten the key and value struct fields into columns:



Code
stage1b <- stage1 |>
  unnest_wider(event_params) |> # expands key, value into columns
  unnest_wider(value, names_sep = "_") |> # expands value struct sub-fields
  unnest_wider(value_1)
cat("After Stage 1b:", nrow(stage1b), "rows x", ncol(stage1b), "cols\n") #15 rows x 7 cols


After Stage 1b: 15 rows x 7 cols


Code
stage1b



  




We now have key, string_value, int_value, float_value, double_value as separate columns. But each row has only one non-NULL value across the four typed columns.



5.2.4 Stage 2: coalesce() — Consolidate the Value Types






ImportantThis Step Is Not in R4DS



coalesce() returns the first non-NULL value across its arguments. Here we use it to merge the four typed value columns into a single value column. This is a GA4-specific requirement not demonstrated in R4DS Chapter 23.




Code
stage2 <- stage1b |>
  mutate(
    value = coalesce(
      string_value,
      as.character(int_value),
      as.character(float_value),
      as.character(double_value)
    )
  ) |>
  select(event_id, event_name, key, value) # Keep only what we need

cat("After Stage 2:", nrow(stage2), "rows x", ncol(stage2), "cols\n")


After Stage 2: 15 rows x 4 cols


Code
stage2



  




Now we have a clean long-format table: one row per parameter per event, with a single value column.



5.2.5 Stage 3: pivot_wider() — Reshape to Analytical Format

The final step converts from long format (one row per parameter) to wide format (one column per parameter), giving us the flat, analysis-ready structure:



Code
stage3 <- stage2 |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = key,
    values_from = value,
    values_fn = first # If a key appears more than once, take the first
  )

cat("After Stage 3:", nrow(stage3), "rows x", ncol(stage3), "cols\n") #2 rows x 8 cols


After Stage 3: 2 rows x 8 cols


Code
stage3



  




We now have one row per event and one column per parameter — exactly the flat structure we need for analysis.

title: Rectangling GA4 Hierarchical Data: Part I - Understanding GA4 Data
section: 5.3 Applying the Full Pipeline to Real GA4 Data
text:
5.3 Applying the Full Pipeline to Real GA4 Data

Now let us apply the same three-stage pipeline to the real ga4 data:



Code
event_params_wide <- ga4 |>
  # Keep only the columns we need before unnesting
  select(
    event_date,
    event_name,
    user_pseudo_id,
    event_timestamp,
    event_params
  ) |>
  unnest_longer(event_params) |> #stage 1
  unnest_wider(event_params) # stage 1b

event_params_wide_flat <- event_params_wide |>
  unnest_wider(value, names_sep = "_") |> # → value_string_value, value_int_value, etc.
  unnest_wider(value_1)

# ── Stage 2: Consolidate the four typed value columns into one ─────────────
event_params_wide_flat <- event_params_wide_flat |>
  mutate(
    param_value = coalesce(
      string_value,
      as.character(int_value),
      as.character(float_value),
      as.character(double_value)
    )
  ) |>
  select(
    event_date,
    event_name,
    user_pseudo_id,
    event_timestamp,
    key,
    param_value
  ) |>

  # ── Stage 3: Pivot from long to wide (one column per parameter) ────────────
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = key,
    values_from = param_value,
    values_fn = first
  )

text:
5.4 Discovering All Available Event Parameters
Before diving into analysis, it helps to know what parameters are available:


Code
param_inventory <- ga4 |>
  select(event_name, event_params) |>
  unnest_longer(event_params) |>
  unnest_wider(event_params) |>
  count(event_name, key, name = "n_occurrences", sort = TRUE) |>
  group_by(key) |>
  mutate(total = sum(n_occurrences)) |>
  ungroup()

# Most common parameters overall
param_inventory |>
  group_by(key) |>
  summarise(total_occurrences = sum(n_occurrences), .groups = "drop") |>
  arrange(desc(total_occurrences)) |>
  slice_head(n = 20) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Top 20 Most Common event_params Keys",
    subtitle = "Helps you know which parameters are available for analysis"
  ) |>
  fmt_number(columns = total_occurrences, decimals = 0) |>
  cols_label(
    key = "Parameter Key",
    total_occurrences = "Total Occurrences"
  ) |>
  opt_stylize(style = 1)

Which parameters appear for which event types?
# Heatmap-style table: parameters × event types
param_inventory |>
  filter(
    # Focus on the most analytically useful event types
    event_name %in%
      c(
        "page_view",
        "purchase",
        "add_to_cart",
        "view_item",
        "begin_checkout",

      ),
    # And the most common parameters
    key %in%
      c(
        "page_location",
        "ga_session_id",
        "ga_session_number",
        "session_engaged",
        "transaction_id",
        "value",
        "currency",

        "page_referrer",

  select(key, event_name, present) |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = event_name,
    values_from = present,
    values_fill = ""
  ) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Parameter Availability by Event Type",
    subtitle = "✓ = parameter present for this event type"
  ) |>

text:
5.5 user_properties: Same Pattern, Different Column

GA4’s user_properties column follows the same key-value struct pattern as event_params. The only difference is what the keys represent — instead of event-level attributes, they capture persistent user-level attributes like user_engagement_type, lifetime_value_bundle, or custom dimensions you define in the GA4 interface.



Code
# Confirm the structure is identical to event_params

ga4 |> names()


 [1] "event_date"      "event_timestamp" "event_name"      "event_params"   
 [5] "user_pseudo_id"  "device"          "geo"             "traffic_source" 
 [9] "ecommerce"       "items"           "user_properties"


Code
ga4 |> select(event_date, event_name, user_properties)



  



Code
ga4 |>
  filter(!map_lgl(user_properties, is.null)) |>
  slice(1) |>
  pull(user_properties) |>
  pluck(1)



  





Flatten user_properties using the same pipeline
ga4$user_properties |> head(10)


[[1]]
# A tibble: 0 × 2
# ℹ 2 variables: key <dbl>, value <list>

[[2]]
# A tibble: 0 × 2
# ℹ 2 variables: key <dbl>, value <list>

[[3]]
# A tibble: 0 × 2
# ℹ 2 variables: key <dbl>, value <list>

[[4]]
# A tibble: 0 × 2
# ℹ 2 variables: key <dbl>, value <list>

[[5]]
# A tibble: 0 × 2
# ℹ 2 variables: key <dbl>, value <list>

[[6]]
# A tibble: 0 × 2
# ℹ 2 variables: key <dbl>, value <list>

[[7]]
# A tibble: 0 × 2

[[8]]
# A tibble: 0 × 2

[[9]]
# A tibble: 0 × 2
# ℹ 2 variables: key <dbl>, value <list>

[[10]]
# A tibble: 0 × 2
# ℹ 2 variables: key <dbl>, value <list>


Flatten user_properties using the same pipeline
ga4 |>
  mutate(
    props_status = case_when(
      map_lgl(user_properties, is.null) ~ "NULL",
      map_lgl(user_properties, \(x) nrow(x) == 0) ~ "empty tibble (0 rows)",
      map_lgl(user_properties, \(x) nrow(x) > 0) ~ "has data",
      TRUE ~ "other"
    )
  ) |>
  count(props_status)



  



Flatten user_properties using the same pipeline
# Diagnose

ga4 |>
  filter(!map_lgl(user_properties, \(x) is.null(x) || nrow(x) == 0)) |>
  slice(1) |>
  pull(user_properties) |>
  pluck(1) |>
  str(max.level = 5)


tibble [1 × 2] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
 $ key  : num NA
 $ value:List of 1
  ..$ :List of 5
  .. ..$ string_value        : num NA
  .. ..$ int_value           : num NA
  .. ..$ float_value         : num NA
  .. ..$ double_value        : num NA
  .. ..$ set_timestamp_micros: num NA


Flatten user_properties using the same pipeline
# User properties is mostly empty
user_props_wide <- ga4 |>
  select(user_pseudo_id, event_date, user_properties) |>
  # Remove rows where user_properties is NULL or empty
  filter(!map_lgl(user_properties, \(x) is.null(x) || nrow(x) == 0)) |>
  distinct(user_pseudo_id, .keep_all = TRUE) |> # One record per user
  unnest_longer(user_properties) |>
  unnest_wider(user_properties) |>
  unnest_wider(value, names_sep = "_") |>
  unnest_wider(value_1) |>
  mutate(
    prop_value = coalesce(
      as.character(string_value),
      as.character(int_value),
      as.character(float_value),
      as.character(double_value)
    )
  ) |>
  select(user_pseudo_id, event_date, key, prop_value) |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = key,
    values_from = prop_value,
    values_fn = first
  )

glimpse(user_props_wide)


Rows: 2
Columns: 3
$ user_pseudo_id <chr> "4941428.2801084311", "35284549.8911315379"
$ event_date     <chr> "20210103", "20210103"
$ `NA`           <chr> NA, NA








NotePattern 3 Summary



The key-value struct pattern requires three stages that build on each other:
List-column of tibbles          (event_params before)
   │
   ▼  unnest_longer()           → Long format: one row per parameter
   │
   ▼  unnest_wider() ×2         → Columns for key + value sub-fields
   │
   ▼  coalesce()                → Single unified value column
   │
   ▼  pivot_wider()             → Wide format: one column per parameter
   │
Flat rectangular tibble         (event_params after)
This pipeline applies identically to event_params and user_properties.








Warninguser_properties Contains No Usable Data in This Dataset



After filtering and inspecting user_properties, we find that this field contains no meaningful data in the GA4 public dataset:
# Summary of user_properties across all rows
# ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
# │ NULL or structurally empty    ~34,000 rows (99.99%)  │
# │ Shell exists but all NA             3 rows  (0.01%)  │
# │ Contains actual property data       0 rows  (0.00%)  │
# └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Why is this field empty?
user_properties stores custom user-scoped attributes that must be explicitly configured in the GA4 property settings and sent by the website’s tracking code. They are not collected automatically.
The Google Merchandise Store public dataset does not populate this field, and Google’s obfuscation process removed what little data existed for privacy protection.
What would real user_properties look like?
In a real GA4 implementation, a marketing team might configure custom user properties like these:



Key
Example Value
Business Use




membership_tier
"gold"
Loyalty program segment


ltv_segment
"high_value"
Lifetime value bucket


subscription_status
"active"
Subscription state


customer_since_year
"2019"
Tenure segment


preferred_category
"apparel"
Affinity segment



The rectangling pipeline still applies
Even though this dataset has no data, the structure of user_properties is identical to event_params — it uses the same key-value struct pattern. The same three-stage pipeline applies:
# The pipeline for user_properties (identical pattern to event_params)
# Stage 1: unnest_longer()     — expand array to one row per property
# Stage 2: unnest_wider()      — expand key and value struct
# Stage 3: coalesce()          — consolidate four typed value columns
# Stage 4: pivot_wider()       — one column per property key

# In a dataset with real user_properties data, this would produce:
# user_pseudo_id | membership_tier | ltv_segment | subscription_status
# u001           | gold            | high_value  | active
# u002           | silver          | mid_value   | inactive
We skip user_properties analysis for the rest of this course and focus on event_params and items, which contain rich data in this dataset.

Task
Code




Start session
con <- dbConnect(duckdb()) then CREATE VIEW ga4 AS ...


Count events
SELECT event_name, COUNT(*) FROM ga4 GROUP BY 1


Filter by date
WHERE event_date BETWEEN '20210101' AND '20210131'


Unnest event_params

Access nested field
device.category, geo.country


End session

The key principle: BigQuery is touched once, everything else is local. DuckDB reads only the Parquet partitions your WHERE event_date clause needs, so a one-week query on 3 months of data only scans ~23% of the files.

section: 2.2 2b. BigQuery SQL — flatten GA4 nested structs
text:
2.2 2b. BigQuery SQL — flatten GA4 nested structs

GA4 data is heavily nested (events → event_params, items, etc.). We unnest the key fields we need in SQL before pulling.



Code
library(glue)

sql <- glue(
  "
  SELECT
    -- Session / user dims
    PARSE_DATE('%Y%m%d', event_date) AS event_date,
    user_pseudo_id,
    (SELECT value.int_value
       FROM UNNEST(event_params)
      WHERE key = 'ga_session_id') AS session_id,

    -- Traffic source
    traffic_source.source AS source,
    traffic_source.medium AS medium,
    traffic_source.name AS campaign,

    -- Device
    device.category AS device_category,
    device.operating_system AS os,

    -- Geo
    geo.country AS country,

    -- Event
    event_name,
    event_timestamp,

    -- Ecommerce (NULL for non-purchase events)
    ecommerce.purchase_revenue AS revenue,
    ecommerce.transaction_id AS transaction_id

  FROM `{BQ_PROJECT}.{BQ_DATASET}.events_*`
  WHERE _TABLE_SUFFIX BETWEEN '{bq_start}' AND '{bq_end}'
    AND event_name IN (
      'session_start', 'page_view',
      'view_item', 'add_to_cart', 'purchase'
    )
"
)

cat(sql)


SELECT
  -- Session / user dims
  PARSE_DATE('%Y%m%d', event_date) AS event_date,
  user_pseudo_id,
  (SELECT value.int_value
     FROM UNNEST(event_params)
    WHERE key = 'ga_session_id') AS session_id,

  -- Traffic source
  traffic_source.source AS source,
  traffic_source.medium AS medium,
  traffic_source.name AS campaign,

  -- Device
  device.category AS device_category,
  device.operating_system AS os,

  -- Geo
  geo.country AS country,

  -- Event
  event_name,
  event_timestamp,

  -- Ecommerce (NULL for non-purchase events)
  ecommerce.purchase_revenue AS revenue,
  ecommerce.transaction_id AS transaction_id

FROM `bigquery-public-data.ga4_obfuscated_sample_ecommerce.events_*`
WHERE _TABLE_SUFFIX BETWEEN '20210101' AND '20210131'
  AND event_name IN (
    'session_start', 'page_view',
    'view_item', 'add_to_cart', 'purchase'
  )

title: GA4 BigQuery Analysis with Arrow & DuckDB
text:
4.2 4b. Channel performance


Code
channels <- dbGetQuery(
  duck,
  "
  WITH sessions AS (
    SELECT
      source,
      medium,
      user_pseudo_id || CAST(session_id AS VARCHAR) AS session_key
    FROM ga4
    WHERE event_name = 'session_start'
  ),
  purchases AS (
    SELECT
      source,
      medium,
      COUNT(DISTINCT transaction_id) AS transactions,
      SUM(revenue)                   AS revenue
    FROM ga4
    WHERE event_name = 'purchase'
    GROUP BY source, medium
  )
  SELECT
    s.source,
    s.medium,
    COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_key)         AS sessions,
    COALESCE(p.transactions, 0)           AS transactions,
    ROUND(COALESCE(p.revenue, 0), 2)      AS revenue,
    ROUND(100.0 * COALESCE(p.transactions, 0)
          / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT s.session_key), 0), 2) AS cvr_pct
  FROM sessions s
  LEFT JOIN purchases p USING (source, medium)
  GROUP BY s.source, s.medium, p.transactions, p.revenue
  ORDER BY sessions DESC
  LIMIT 20
"
)

title: Guideline for Working with GA4 Data in R
text:
1.1 Small GA4 Project (< 7 days)
For your current 7-day project, RDS alone is enough. As you scale up or start wanting SQL-style queries, DuckDB + Parquet is the most powerful pairing — DuckDB queries the Parquet files directly without even needing to load them first.


1.1.1 A few things worth highlighting from the table:
The nested column problem is the deciding factor for GA4 specifically. RDS and DuckDB both handle event_params, device, and geo as-is, while raw Parquet forces you to flatten them first (which is where all that purrr complexity came from).
DuckDB + Parquet is the best long-term investment because DuckDB can query Parquet files on disk directly with a single line — duckdb::duckdb_read_csv() or FROM read_parquet('data/*.parquet') — meaning you get the storage efficiency of Parquet without writing Arrow query pipelines.
For your current 7-day project, RDS wins on simplicity. But if you plan to scale to months of GA4 data, switching to DuckDB early costs very little effort and will save you a lot of pain later.
crumbs: Guideline for Working with GA4 Data in R

title: Guideline for Working with GA4 Data in R
text:
1.2 Large GA4 Data (90 days)


1.2.1 The three key shifts from the 7-day analysis are:
RDS is now off the table. At 3 months, you’re likely looking at 3–10M rows and 500MB–2GB of raw data. Loading that entirely into RAM on every session is slow, risks crashing R with an out-of-memory error, and gives you no ability to query just a slice of the data.
DuckDB alone becomes marginal. A .duckdb file is a single binary blob — you can’t selectively read January without touching February and March. It’s fine for medium data but loses its edge at this scale.
DuckDB + Parquet is the clear winner, and the setup is simpler than it sounds. You save once as date-partitioned Parquet files, then DuckDB reads them directly on disk:
```{r}
library(duckdb)
con <- dbConnect(duckdb())

# DuckDB reads Parquet files directly — no loading into R first
dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT
    event_date,
    event_name,
    ep.value.string_value AS page_location
  FROM read_parquet('data/ga4_parquet/**/*.parquet', hive_partitioning = true)
  CROSS JOIN UNNEST(event_params) AS ep(key, value)
  WHERE event_date BETWEEN '20210101' AND '20210131'
    AND ep.key = 'page_location'
")
```






Three things make this powerful at your scale: the hive_partitioning = true flag means DuckDB skips entire month folders when your WHERE clause doesn’t need them; UNNEST handles the nested GA4 columns without any upfront flattening; and nothing is loaded into R memory until dbGetQuery() returns the final filtered result.
crumbs: Guideline for Working with GA4 Data in R

install.packages(c("bigrquery", "duckdb", "arrow", "dplyr"))

title: Guideline for Working with GA4 Data in R
section: 2.2 Step 2: Download from BigQuery once, save as partitioned Parquet
text:
2.2 Step 2: Download from BigQuery once, save as partitioned Parquet
Do this once only. All future analysis reads from local Parquet files.
```{r}
library(bigrquery)
library(arrow)
library(dplyr)

billing_project <- "your-project-id"

# Download in monthly chunks to avoid memory pressure
months <- list(
  c("20210101", "20210131"),
  c("20210201", "20210228"),
  c("20210301", "20210331")
)

dir.create("data/ga4_parquet", recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

for (m in months) {
  message("Downloading ", m[1], " to ", m[2], "...")
  
  result <- bq_project_query(billing_project, glue::glue("
    SELECT
      event_date, event_timestamp, event_name,
      event_params, user_pseudo_id,
      device, geo, traffic_source, ecommerce, items
    FROM `bigquery-public-data.ga4_obfuscated_sample_ecommerce.events_*`
    WHERE _TABLE_SUFFIX BETWEEN '{m[1]}' AND '{m[2]}'
  "))
  
  chunk <- bq_table_download(result, bigint = "numeric", page_size = 1000)
  
  # Write directly to partitioned Parquet — Arrow infers event_date partition
  chunk |>
    group_by(event_date) |>
    write_dataset("data/ga4_parquet", format = "parquet")
  
  rm(chunk); gc()  # free memory between chunks
}
```

Your folder structure will look like:

data/ga4_parquet/
  event_date=20210101/part-0.parquet
  event_date=20210102/part-0.parquet
  ...
crumbs: Guideline for Working with GA4 Data in R

section: 2.3 Step 3: Connect DuckDB to the Parquet files
text:
2.3 Step 3: Connect DuckDB to the Parquet files
At the start of every future session, just run this — no re-downloading, no loading into RAM:
```{r}
library(duckdb)
library(DBI)

con <- dbConnect(duckdb())

# Register the entire partitioned dataset as a virtual table
dbExecute(con, "
  CREATE VIEW ga4 AS
  SELECT * FROM read_parquet(
    'data/ga4_parquet/**/*.parquet',
    hive_partitioning = true
  )
")
```
From here, ga4 behaves like a database table. DuckDB reads only the Parquet partitions your query needs.
crumbs: Guideline for Working with GA4 Data in R

title: Guideline for Working with GA4 Data in R
text:
2.4 Step 4: Query as needed
Flat columns — standard SQL:
```{r}
# Only touches January partitions on disk
dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT event_date, event_name, COUNT(*) AS n
  FROM ga4
  WHERE event_date BETWEEN '20210101' AND '20210131'
  GROUP BY 1, 2
  ORDER BY 3 DESC
")
```
Nested columns — use UNNEST in SQL:
```{r}
# Extract page_location from event_params without any upfront flattening
dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT
    event_date,
    user_pseudo_id,
    ep.value.string_value AS page_location
  FROM ga4
  CROSS JOIN UNNEST(event_params) AS ep(key, value)
  WHERE event_name = 'page_view'
    AND ep.key = 'page_location'
    AND event_date BETWEEN '20210101' AND '20210131'
")
```
Device and geo nested fields:
```{r}
dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT
    device.category AS device_type,
    device.operating_system AS os,
    geo.country,
    geo.city,
    COUNT(DISTINCT user_pseudo_id) AS users
  FROM ga4
  WHERE event_date BETWEEN '20210101' AND '20210331'
  GROUP BY 1, 2, 3, 4
  ORDER BY 5 DESC
")
```
Purchase funnel — joining unnested params:
```{r}
dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT
    event_name,
    COUNT(DISTINCT user_pseudo_id) AS users,
    COUNT(*)                        AS events
  FROM ga4
  WHERE event_name IN ('session_start','view_item','add_to_cart','purchase')
    AND event_date BETWEEN '20210101' AND '20210331'
  GROUP BY 1
  ORDER BY 3 DESC
")

```

text:
1.1 Packages to use


Code
#install.packages("repurrrsive")
library(tidyverse)
library(repurrrsive)

title: Rectangling Hierarchical Data: Ch23
text:
2.1 Hierarchy


Lists can contain any type of object, including other lists.
This makes them suitable for representing hierarchical (tree-like) structures:




Code
x3 <- list(list(1, 2), list(3, 4))
str(x3)


List of 2
 $ :List of 2
  ..$ : num 1
  ..$ : num 2
 $ :List of 2
  ..$ : num 3
  ..$ : num 4




This is notably different to c(), which generates a flat vector:




Code
c(c(1, 2), c(3, 4))


[1] 1 2 3 4




Code
x5 <- list(1, list(2, list(3, list(4, list(5)))))
str(x5)


List of 2
 $ : num 1
 $ :List of 2
  ..$ : num 2
  ..$ :List of 2
  .. ..$ : num 3
  .. ..$ :List of 2
  .. .. ..$ : num 4
  .. .. ..$ :List of 1
  .. .. .. ..$ : num 5


Code
view(x5)

title: Rectangling Hierarchical Data: Ch23
text:
2.2 List-Columns


Code
df <- tibble(
  x = 1:2,
  y = c("a", "b"),
  z = list(list(1, 2), list(3, 4, 5))
)
df



  





Code
df |>
  filter(x == 1)



  





Computing with list-columns is harder.




tibble() treats lists like vectors and the print method has been designed with lists in mind.

title: Rectangling Hierarchical Data: Ch23
text:
3.1 Named list-columns


Named list-columns naturally unnest into columns: each named element becomes a new named column.




Code
df1 <- tribble(
  ~x , ~y                   ,
   1 , list(a = 11, b = 12) ,
   2 , list(a = 21, b = 22) ,
   3 , list(a = 31, b = 32) ,
)
df1

title: Rectangling Hierarchical Data: Ch23
text:
3.2 Unnamed list-columns


When the children are unnamed, the number of elements tends to vary from row-to-row.




Unnamed list-columns naturally unnest into rows: you’ll get one row for each child.




Code
df2 <- tribble(
  ~x , ~y               ,
   1 , list(11, 12, 13) ,
   2 , list(21)         ,
   3 , list(31, 32)     ,
)

title: Rectangling Hierarchical Data: Ch23
text:
3.3 unnest_wider()

Used when each row has the same number of elements with the same names



Code
df1 |>
  unnest_wider(y)



  




By default, the names of the new columns come exclusively from the names of the list elements, but you can use the names_sep argument to request that they combine the column name and the element name. This is useful for disambiguating repeated names.



Code
df1 |>
  unnest_wider(y, names_sep = "_")

title: Rectangling Hierarchical Data: Ch23
text:
3.4 unnest_longer()

Used when each row contains an unnamed list



Code
df2 |>
  unnest_longer(y)



  




Note how x is duplicated for each element inside of y: we get one row of output for each element inside the list-column.


But what happens if one of the elements is empty, as in the following example?



Code
df6 <- tribble(
  ~x  , ~y         ,
  "a" , list(1, 2) ,
  "b" , list(3)    ,
  "c" , list()
)

df6 |>
  unnest_longer(y)



  




We get zero rows in the output, so the row effectively disappears.


If you want to preserve that row, adding NA in y, set keep_empty = TRUE.



Code
df6 <- tribble(
  ~x  , ~y         ,
  "a" , list(1, 2) ,
  "b" , list(3)    ,
  "c" , list(NA)
)

df6 |>
  unnest_longer(y, keep_empty = TRUE)

title: Rectangling Hierarchical Data: Ch23
text:
3.5 Inconsitent Types

What happens if you unnest a list-column that contains different types of vector?



Code
df4 <- tribble(
  ~x  , ~y                 ,
  "a" , list(1)            ,
  "b" , list("a", TRUE, 5)
)




Code
df4 |>
  unnest_longer(y)



  




The output contains a list-column, but every element of the list-column contains a single element.


Dealing with inconsistent types is challenging and the details depend on the precise nature of the problem and your goals, but you’ll most likely need tools from Iteration.

title: Rectangling Hierarchical Data: Ch23
text:
3.6 Other functions from tidyr

unnest_auto() automatically picks between unnest_longer() and unnest_wider() based on the structure of the list-column. It’s great for rapid exploration, but ultimately it’s a bad idea because it doesn’t force you to understand how your data is structured, and makes your code harder to understand.
unnest() expands both rows and columns. It’s useful when you have a list-column that contains a 2d structure like a data frame, which you don’t see in this book, but you might encounter if you use the tidymodels ecosystem.

title: Rectangling Hierarchical Data: Ch23
text:
3.7 Exercises

What happens when you use unnest_wider() with unnamed list-columns like df2? What argument is now necessary? What happens to missing values?
What happens when you use unnest_longer() with named list-columns like df1? What additional information do you get in the output? How can you suppress that extra detail?
From time-to-time you encounter data frames with multiple list-columns with aligned values. For example, in the following data frame, the values of y and z are aligned (i.e. y and z will always have the same length within a row, and the first value of y corresponds to the first value of z). What happens if you apply two unnest_longer() calls to this data frame? How can you preserve the relationship between x and y? (Hint: carefully read the docs).



Code
df4 <- tribble(
  ~x  , ~y                              , ~z                              ,
  "a" , list("y-a-1", "y-a-2")          , list("z-a-1", "z-a-2")          ,
  "b" , list("y-b-1", "y-b-2", "y-b-3") , list("z-b-1", "z-b-2", "z-b-3")
)

text:
4.4 What Is hoist()?
hoist() is a function from {tidyr} that lets you pull specific elements out of a list-column by name or position, without unnesting the entire structure. Think of it as a surgical tool compared to the blunt instruments of unnest_wider() and unnest_longer()

title: Rectangling Hierarchical Data: Ch23
Rectangling Hierarchical Data: Ch23
text:
5.1 Data Types
It has six key data types. Four of them are scalars:

The simplest type is a null (null) which plays the same role as NA in R. It represents the absence of data.
A string is much like a string in R, but must always use double quotes.
A number is similar to R’s numbers: they can use integer (e.g., 123), decimal (e.g., 123.45), or scientific (e.g., 1.23e3) notation. JSON doesn’t support Inf, -Inf, or NaN.
A boolean is similar to R’s TRUE and FALSE, but uses lowercase true and false.

To represent multiple values you need to use one of the two remaining types: arrays and objects.

Both arrays and objects are similar to lists in R; the difference is whether or not they’re named.

An array is like an unnamed list, and is written with []. For example [1, 2, 3] is an array containing 3 numbers, and [null, 1, "string", false] is an array that contains a null, a number, a string, and a boolean.
An object is like a named list, and is written with {}. The names (keys in JSON terminology) are strings, so must be surrounded by quotes. For example, {"x": 1, "y": 2} is an object that maps x to 1 and y to 2.

title: Rectangling Hierarchical Data: Ch23
text:
5.2 jsonlite

This package convert JSON into R data structures
read_json() and parse_json(). 



Code
# A path to a json file inside the package:
gh_users_json()


[1] "C:/Users/jmjung/AppData/Local/Programs/R/R-4.5.2/library/repurrrsive/extdata/gh_users.json"

# Check it's the same as the data we were using previously
identical(gh_users, gh_users2)


[1] TRUE


Code
#> [1] TRUE




Code
str(parse_json("1"))


 int 1




Code

List of 3
 $ : int 1
 $ : int 2
 $ : int 3




Code
str(parse_json('{"x": [1, 2, 3]}')) # object


List of 1
 $ x:List of 3
  ..$ : int 1
  ..$ : int 2
  ..$ : int 3

0.3 Pull Data from BigQuery → Single Parquet File


Code
# ── Run this chunk ONCE. It saves data locally. ───────────────────────────────
dir.create("data", showWarnings = FALSE)

query_result <- bq_project_query(
  GCP_PROJECT,
  "SELECT
     event_date,
     event_timestamp,
     event_name,
     event_params,
     user_pseudo_id,
     device,
     geo,
     traffic_source,
     ecommerce,
     items,
     user_properties
   FROM `bigquery-public-data.ga4_obfuscated_sample_ecommerce.events_*`
   WHERE _TABLE_SUFFIX BETWEEN '20210101' AND '20210107'"
)

# A tibble data residing in memory
ga4_raw <- query_result |>
  bq_table_download(
    bigint = "numeric", # Convert INT64 to R numeric (sufficient for most analyses)
    page_size = 1000 # Download in batches of 10k rows
  )

# Save once after BigQuery download
# install.packages("qs2")
library(qs2)

# Save
qs_save(ga4_raw, "data/ga4_raw.qs2")

# Disconnect from BigQuery — we don't need it anymore
dbDisconnect(bq_con)

title: Rectangling GA4 Hierarchical Data: Part II - Focusing on Marketing Insights
text:
0.4 Load the data to RAM


Code
library(qs2)
# Create or load an Arrow table
ga4_qs <- qs_read("data/ga4_raw.qs2")








Noteqs Package



qs stands for Quick Serialization. It is a drop-in replacement for saveRDS()/readRDS() that uses a more efficient compression algorithm (ZSTD) instead of R’s default gzip, making it:

2-5x faster to save and load
Smaller file sizes than RDS
Preserves all R objects exactly, including nested lists, data frames, and complex structures

It was designed specifically for the use case of caching large R objects locally — which is exactly what we need for GA4 data.
qs2 is the newer, updated version with further performance improvements.

title: Rectangling GA4 Hierarchical Data: Part II - Focusing on Marketing Insights
text:
1.1 Building the Master Analytical Table
Rather than re-running the pipelines for every analysis, we assemble one wide, analysis-ready table that joins the flattened structs, flattened event_params, and e-commerce data:


Code
# ── Step 1: Flatten struct columns ────────────────────────────────────────────
ga4_structs <- ga4_qs |>
  filter(event_name == "purchase") |>
  select(
    event_date,
    event_name,
    event_timestamp,
    user_pseudo_id,
    device,
    geo,
    traffic_source
  ) |>
  unnest_wider(device, names_sep = "_") |>
  unnest_wider(geo, names_sep = "_") |>
  unnest_wider(traffic_source, names_sep = "_")
names(ga4_structs)

Code
# ── Step 2: Flatten event_params ──────────────────────────────────────────────
ga4_params <- ga4_qs |>
  select(
    event_date,
    event_name,
    event_timestamp,
    user_pseudo_id,
    event_params
  ) |>
  unnest_longer(event_params) |>
  unnest_wider(event_params) |>
  unnest_wider(value, names_sep = "_") |>
  unnest_wider(value_1) |>
  mutate(
    param_value = coalesce(
      string_value,
      as.character(int_value),
      as.character(float_value),
      as.character(double_value)
    )
  ) |>
  select(
    event_date,
    event_name,
    event_timestamp,
    user_pseudo_id,
    key,
    param_value
  ) |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = key,
    values_from = param_value,
    values_fn = first
  )

# Save
qs_save(ga4_params, "data/ga4_params.qs2")

      origin = "1970-01-01",
      tz = "America/Los_Angeles" # GA4 obfuscated dataset is in US/Pacific

  )

text:
1.3 Analysis 2: Device and Geographic Breakdown
Marketing Question: Where are users coming from, and on what devices?


Code
# Daily sessions by device category
daily_device <- ga4_master |>
  filter(event_name == "session_start") |>
  count(event_date, device_category) |>
  mutate(device_category = str_to_title(device_category))

# Geographic distribution — top countries
top_countries <- ga4_master |>
  filter(event_name == "session_start") |>
  count(geo_country, sort = TRUE) |>
  filter(!is.na(geo_country)) |>
  slice_head(n = 15) |>
  mutate(
    pct = n / sum(n),
    geo_country = fct_reorder(geo_country, n)
  )

# Browser share (for device-specific targeting decisions)
browser_share <- ga4_master |>
  filter(event_name == "session_start") |>
  count(device_browser, device_category) |>
  filter(!is.na(device_browser)) |>
  group_by(device_category) |>
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) |>
  slice_max(pct, n = 5) |>
  ungroup()

text:
1.6 Analysis 5: Session Engagement Deep-Dive
Marketing Question: Are sessions actually engaging, and which channels drive the most engaged sessions?
This combines event_params extraction (Pattern 3) with struct unnesting (Pattern 1) in a single pipeline:


Code
session_engagement <- ga4_master |>
  filter(event_name == "session_start") |>
  mutate(
    # session_engaged comes from event_params (Pattern 3 — already flattened)
    is_engaged = session_engaged == "1",
    ga_session_num = as.integer(ga_session_number),
    # device_category from device struct (Pattern 1 — already flattened)
    device = str_to_title(device_category),
    # traffic_source_medium from traffic_source struct (Pattern 1)
    channel = case_when(
      is.na(traffic_source_medium) |
        traffic_source_medium == "(none)" ~ "Direct",
      traffic_source_medium == "organic" ~ "Organic Search",
      traffic_source_medium %in%
        c("cpc", "ppc", "paid") ~ "Paid Search",
      str_detect(traffic_source_medium, "email") ~ "Email",
      str_detect(traffic_source_medium, "social") ~ "Social",
      TRUE ~ str_to_title(traffic_source_medium)
    )
  ) |>
  group_by(channel, device) |>
  summarise(
    sessions = n(),
    engaged_sessions = sum(is_engaged, na.rm = TRUE),
    engagement_rate = mean(is_engaged, na.rm = TRUE),
    new_user_sessions = sum(ga_session_num == 1, na.rm = TRUE),
    returning_sessions = sum(ga_session_num > 1, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(sessions >= 20) # Filter to statistically meaningful segments

session_engagement



  





Code
session_engagement |>
  ggplot(aes(
    x = device,
    y = fct_reorder(channel, engagement_rate),
    fill = engagement_rate
  )) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = percent(engagement_rate, accuracy = 1)),
    fontface = "bold",
    size = 4
  ) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#d32f2f",
    mid = "#fff176",
    high = "#388e3c",
    midpoint = 0.55,
    labels = percent,
    name = "Engagement\nRate"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Session Engagement Rate by Channel × Device",
    subtitle = glue(
      "Engagement rate = % of sessions meeting Google's engagement criteria\n",
      "(session_engaged parameter from event_params — Pattern 3 rectangling)"
    ),
    x = "Device Category",
    y = "Traffic Channel"
  ) +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 11)
  )








TipWhat is an “Engaged Session” in GA4?



GA4 defines an engaged session as one that meets at least one of:

Lasts longer than 10 seconds
Has at least 2 page views or screen views
Has a conversion event

The session_engaged parameter (value "1" or "0") in event_params is how GA4 records this classification. We extracted it using the Pattern 3 pipeline from Part 2.

4.1 What We Covered
This document extended R4DS Chapter 23 in two important directions:

4.1.1 From R4DS (Applied to GA4)







Tool
GA4 Use Case




unnest_wider()
Flatten device, geo, traffic_source, ecommerce struct columns


unnest_longer()
Expand items array and first-stage expansion of event_params


hoist()
Fast targeted extraction of specific struct fields


Both combined
Fully rectangling the items repeated struct array





4.1.2 Beyond R4DS (GA4-Specific)







Problem
Solution




Key-value struct arrays (event_params)
3-stage pipeline: unnest_longer() → unnest_wider() ×2 → coalesce() → pivot_wider()


Union-type value structs
dplyr::coalesce() across the four typed value columns


BigQuery INT64 types
bigint = "numeric" in bq_table_download()


Scale/performance
Download once → cache as .qs → load locally
